coredata.dc:title: Determination of tetracycline by FRET fluorescence between chenpi carbon quantum dots and copper nanoparticles
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry Chinese Academy of Sciences.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry Chinese Academy of Sciences.
coredata.dc:creator: LU, Ping
HOU, Xiangru
GA, Lu
AODENG, Gerile
AI, Jun
coredata.dc:description: This paper describes a new strategy based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), in which the rapid synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and copper nanoparticles (CuNPs) is used for the determination of tetracycline (TC). Therefore, in this paper, CQDs for fluorescence determination of TC were prepared by microwave heating using chenpi as a carbon source. The prepared CQDs showed good dispersion and uniform spherical shape under transmission electron microscope (TEM), with an average particle size of about 1.91 nm. Under ultraviolet (UV) irradiation, the CQDs emitted bright blue-green fluorescence with a strong fluorescence (> 1.6 × 103 a.u.), and the fluorescence intensity did not change within one week. The emission wavelength of CQDs overlapped with the absorbance of CuNPs, and therefore, CuNPs could burst the fluorescence of CQDs by FRET mechanism. After incubation with TC, the fluorescence of CQDs was restored by the ligand reaction of CuNPs with TC. The assay showed good linearity for TC in the 1–100 nmol/L range, with a determination limit of 0.3554 nmol/L. This study demonstrated the potential of CQDs in antibiotic assays and provided a valuable method for monitoring TC levels.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Copper nanoparticles
Chenpi
Tetracycline

Principle of TC determination by FRET
X-ray diffraction analysis, FT-IR spectral analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for preparation of CQDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs (inset shows CQDs under normal light as well as under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation); (b) Overlap of the emission wavelength of CQDs with the absorbance of CuNPs; (c) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs, CQDs+CuNPs, CQDs+CuNPs+TC.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of photobleaching properties of CQDs at 1 h; (b) Fluorescence scatter plots of photobleaching properties of CQDs at 1 h; (c) Comparison of initial fluorescence intensity of CQDs with fluorescence intensity after one week.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of emission wavelengths of CQDs at different excitation wavelengths; (b) UV-Vis absorption spectra of CQDs.
(a) TEM plots of CQDs; (b) CQDs particle size distribution map.
(a) XRD analysis of CQDs; (b) FT-IR analysis of CQDs.
(a) XPS full range survey spectrum and high-resolution (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, and (d) O 1s spectra of as-prepared CQDs.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs after the addition of different concentrations of TC; (b) Linear relationship between different concentrations of TC and CQDs.

head.ce:title: Determination of tetracycline by FRET fluorescence between chenpi carbon quantum dots and copper nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Inner Mongolia Key Laboratory of Environmental Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Environmental Science, Inner Mongolia Normal University, Hohhot 010022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Inner Mongolia Key Laboratory of Environmental Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Environmental Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aCollege of Chemistry and Environmental Science, Inner Mongolia Key Laboratory of Environmental Chemistry, Inner Mongolia Normal University, 81 zhaowudalu, Hohhot 010022, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmacy, Inner Mongolia Medical University, Hohhot 010110, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bCollege of Pharmacy, Inner Mongolia Medical University, Jinchuankaifaqu, Hohhot 010110, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper describes a new strategy based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), in which the rapid synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and copper nanoparticles (CuNPs) is used for the determination of tetracycline (TC). Therefore, in this paper, CQDs for fluorescence determination of TC were prepared by microwave heating using chenpi as a carbon source. The prepared CQDs showed good dispersion and uniform spherical shape under transmission electron microscope (TEM), with an average particle size of about 1.91 nm. Under ultraviolet (UV) irradiation, the CQDs emitted bright blue-green fluorescence with a strong fluorescence (> 1.6 × 103 a.u.), and the fluorescence intensity did not change within one week. The emission wavelength of CQDs overlapped with the absorbance of CuNPs, and therefore, CuNPs could burst the fluorescence of CQDs by FRET mechanism. After incubation with TC, the fluorescence of CQDs was restored by the ligand reaction of CuNPs with TC. The assay showed good linearity for TC in the 1–100 nmol/L range, with a determination limit of 0.3554 nmol/L. This study demonstrated the potential of CQDs in antibiotic assays and provided a valuable method for monitoring TC levels.

Schematic illustration for preparation of CQDs.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were first discovered in 2004 while purifying single-walled carbon nanotubes [1]. Carbon quantum dots have nanoscale size, nearly flat or spherical morphology, excellent water solubility, a wide range of ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) light absorption, and rich fluorescent color [2]. In addition, CQDs have a simple synthesis process, low cost, good biocompatibility, excellent fluorescent properties, good electrical conductivity, high chemical stability, environmentally friendly, compared with traditional fluorescent organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, CQDs have good chemical stability and inertia, can form stable colloidal solutions, and have a strong resistance to photobleaching ability [3,4]. Due to their excellent physicochemical properties, CQDs are widely used in bio-imaging [5], photodynamic therapy [6], fluorescence determination [7], and other fields. The synthesis of CQDs has also received extensive attention, and the synthesis of CQDs can be divided into two general categories, the “top-down” method and the “bottom-up” method [8]. “Top-down” synthesis methods use large carbon-rich materials as carbon sources, such as electrochemical synthesis and chemical ablation; while bottom-up methods synthesize CQDs from small precursor molecules, such as hydrothermal synthesis and microwave heating [9]. Currently, how to green synthesize CQDs has received wide attention, and the microwave method is often applied to green synthesize CQDs. The microwave method can be applied to small molecules, biomass does not require an acid catalyst, and water can be used as the reaction medium. Using conventional microwave-assisted carbonization of carbon sources to obtain CQDs with good water solubility and without further passivation is a fast, green, and economical method [10]. In the study of Choi et al. [11], blue fluorescent CQDs were prepared by microwave method using lysine as the precursor and used for bioimaging. In the study of Guo et al. [12], CQDs were prepared from m-phenylenediamine and glucose by microwave heating and used for bioimaging. In this paper, chenpi, a common and readily available Chinese herb, was used as the carbon source to prepare CQDs with blue-green fluorescence under the irradiation of 365 nm ultraviolet light by microwave heating, and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) was constructed with copper nanoparticles (CuNPs) for the determination of tetracycline.

Chenpi is the dried mature fruit peel of orange and its cultivated varieties in the Brassicaceae family. The main medicinal components of tangerine peel are volatile oil and hesperidin. Tangerine peel is rich in inositol, vitamin B, vitamin C, carotenoids, pectin, thymol, and beta-sitosterol. Chenpi is used in combination with other herbs to treat digestive and respiratory disorders such as indigestion, stomach pain, bloating, and coughing. In addition, tangerine peel and its components have the effect of regulating blood lipids, anti-thrombosis, anti-atherosclerosis and other therapeutic effects on cardiovascular diseases, and herbal compounds containing tangerine peel can also be used to treat anorexia nervosa [13].

Tetracyclines (TCs), including oxytetracycline (OTC), tetracycline (TC), and chlortetracycline (CTC) [14]. TC, as one of the most commonly used pharmaceutical compounds, has a wide range of effects on Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria by specifically inhibiting the synthesis of prokaryotic (bacterial) ribosomal proteins and is used as a veterinary drug and livestock growth promoter in aquaculture and livestock operations [15,16]. In addition to its antimicrobial effects, TC has anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive properties [17], e.g., TC can be widely used in the clinical treatment of periodontal disease through the oral drug route [18]. Excess tetracycline can remain in food of animal origin and enter the human body along the food chain, posing a serious threat to human health. EU Decree 675/92 stipulates that the total amount of tetracycline compounds in milk shall not exceed 100 µg/kg, and the Maximum Residue Limit of Veterinary Drugs in Animal Foods issued by the Ministry of Agriculture of China stipulates that the residue limit of hygromycin, TC, and CTC in milk is 100 µg/L.

Metallic nanoparticles (NPs) are typically spherical particles with diameters in the range of 1–100 nm, and NPs have higher surface-to-volume ratios than particles composed of the same material at the non-nanometer scale [19]. NPs have promising applications in catalysis, electronics, optoelectronics, information storage, biosensors, surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS), etc. Compared with bulk materials, NPs have a wide range of functional versatility and can be used as common structural elements or composite materials [20]. While CuNPs are widely used due to their low cost and superiority over precious metal nanoparticles (Ag/AuNPs) in terms of resistance to ionic migration [21]. CuNPs have good optical, antimicrobial, electrical, and catalytic properties and can be used in biosensors, gas sensors, electrochemical sensors, solar/photovoltaic protection, and electronic conductive adhesives (ECAs) fabrication or catalysis and inkjet. It is also considered a strong antimicrobial agent against microorganisms and is also used for the control of fungal diseases in crops as well as an on-farm disinfectant [22,23]. In contrast, sensor methods that co-construct FRETs with CuNPs and CQDs and use them to detect small molecules are not common. Therefore, in this paper, CQDs were used as energy donors, and CuNPs were used as energy acceptors to co-construct the FRET mechanism and used to detect tetracycline.

FRET or resonance energy transfer (RET) is a mechanism that describes the transfer of energy between donors and acceptors of photosensitive chromophores [24,25]. It can be described as a donor body fluorophore in the excited state non-radiatively transferring its excitation energy to a neighboring acceptor fluorophore, reflecting the process of energy transfer between multiple light-responsive chromophores [26,27].

CQDs were prepared by microwave heating using water as the solvent and chenpi with cyclic double bond structure as raw material. The microwave method in the microwave reaction promotes dehydration and carbonation of chenpi, which finally forms CQDs rich in nitrogen and oxygen groups such as C=O, C–O, and C–N. The structure of CQDs was also analyzed by combining characterization means such as Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), UV-Vis spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Based on the characterization results, combined with the literature speculation [28], this paper proposed the formation mechanism of CQDs, i.e., the saturated carbon chain structure in chenpi carbonizes with the increase of reaction time to form carbon nuclei, and the double bonds are opened during the microwave reaction to form saturated carbon chain structure, which is co-modified to the surface of CQDs. With the prolongation of the reaction time, CQDs with abundant surface functional groups were gradually formed.

In this paper, CQDs were prepared by chenpi, the prepared CQDs were used as energy donors, and the prepared CuNPs were used as energy acceptors. When the emission spectra of the CQDs overlapped with the absorption spectra of the CuNPs, a FRET between the CQDs in the excited state and the bursting agent in the ground state would occur, resulting in a fluorescence burst of the prepared CQDs [29]. After the addition of TC, the interaction of TC with the surface groups of CuNPs resulted in the recovery of CQDs fluorescence.

Chenpi (from Beijing Tongrentang (Anguo) Traditional Chinese Medicine Drinking Tablets Corporation) was purchased at a pharmacy, and the water used during the experiments was ultrapure water (18.2 MQ·cm) prepared by Milli. Q ultrapure water system. The main reagents used in the experiments were analytically pure except for tetracycline (C22H24N2O8 Shanghai Klin Biochemical Technology Corporation), which was the Chinese Pharmacopoeia. Glucose (C6H12O6 Tianjin Fengfeng Chemical Reagent Technology Corporation (formerly Tianjin Chemical Reagent Third Factory)). Copper nitrate trihydrate (Cu(NO3)2·3H2O Tianjin Fengfeng Chemical Reagent Technology Corporation (formerly Tianjin Chemical Reagent Third Factory)). Anhydrous ethanol (CH3CH2OH Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Corporation (formerly Tianjin Chemical Reagent Sixth Plant, Third Branch)). Ethylene glycol (HOCH2CH2OH Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Corporation (formerly Tianjin Chemical Reagent Sixth Plant, Third Branch)). Sodium chloride (NaCl Tianjin Beilian Fine Chemicals Development Corporation). Potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4 Tianjin Chemical Reagent Third Factory). Disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4·12H2O Shantou Chemical Reagent Factory). Methanol (CH3OH Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Corporation (formerly Tianjin Chemical Reagent Sixth Plant, Third Branch)).

The experimental instruments were used in the experiments were as follows: F97pro fluorescence spectrometer (Shanghai Prismatic Technology Corporation), U-2900 UV-Vis Spectrometer (Hitachi High-Technologies Corporation), JEOL-2100F transmission electron microscope (Jeol Corporation, Japan), EQ-100DE CNC Ultrasonic Cleaner (Kunshan Ultrasonic Instrument Corporation), P70D21N1P-S1(W0) Microwave Oven (Grants Microwave Oven & Appliance Corporation), K-Alpha XPS, Nicolet iS50 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), XRD analysis using Japanese Regaku Ultima Type IV X-ray diffractometer, D1008 high-speed small centrifuge (Beijing Dalong Xingchuang Experimental Instrument Co., Ltd.).

Grind the purchased chenpi into powder. 0.1 g of the powder was dissolved in 10 mL of ethylene glycol and the mixture was heated in a microwave oven for 2 min. The solution turns yellowish brown, indicating the formation of CQDs. After cooling to room temperature, the liquid supernatant was taken and centrifuged at 10,000 r/min for 10 min, while it was later filtered through a 0.22 µm filter membrane to obtain the further processed solution. The prepared CQDs solution was stored at 4 °C away from light for the next study. Fig. 1 shows a schematic diagram of the preparation of CQDs using chenpi as a precursor.

The preparation was carried out by referring to the method of the lab sister. 1 mmol/L Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, 0.1 mol/L glucose solution, and 0.4 mol/L NaOH solution were prepared separately. The pH of 0.1 mol/L glucose solution was adjusted to 12 with 0.4 mol/L NaOH, and 1 mL of 1 mmol/L Cu(NO3)2·3H2O was added to 10 mL of 0.1 mol/L glucose solution with a pH of 12. The mixture was stirred for 30 min at room temperature and protected from light, after stirring, the mixture was stirred in a water bath at 55 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the liquid was centrifuged for 30 min and then stored at 4 °C and protected from light.

In treating the TC solution, the TC was dissolved in methanol solution (CH3OH) as a stock solution. A further 0.01 M PBS solution with a pH of 7.2 (containing potassium dihydrogen phosphate (10 mmol/L), sodium chloride (150 mmol/L), and disodium hydrogen phosphate (10 mmol/L)) was configured. The TC stock solutions were diluted to different concentrations with PBS standard solution and stored at 4 °C under light protection [30].

CQDs transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were observed on JEOL-2100F (JEOL Corporation, Japan). All fluorescence spectra were obtained using F97pro fluorescence spectrometer. UV-Vis absorption spectra were recorded on the U-2900 (Hitachi High-tech) spectrophotometer. FT-IR spectroscopy uses the Nicolet iS50 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) to investigate the functional groups contained in CQDs. The elemental composition and surface functional groups of CQDs were analyzed by K-Alpha XPS. The phase composition X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis of CQDs was performed on the Regaku Ultima Type IV X-ray diffractometer manufactured in Japan. Ultrasonic uses EQ-100DE CNC ultrasonic cleaner (Kunshan Ultrasonic Instrument Co., Ltd.). Centrifugation using D1008 high-speed small centrifuge (Beijing Dalong Xingchuang Experimental Instrument Co., Ltd.). CQDs were synthesized using a P70D21N1P-S1(W0) microwave oven (Grants Microwave Oven & Appliance Company).

ce:section.ce:section-title: TC determination based on FRET mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: We mentioned above that in this paper, the prepared CQDs are used as energy donors, and CuNPs are used as energy acceptors. During the construction of the FRET mechanism, some parameters were optimized and the best volume ratio was determined. 200 µL of CuNPs were added to 1000 µL of CQDs and stirred at room temperature for 15 min away from light, after which the fluorescence intensity was measured. Then 300 µL of different concentrations of TC was added to the mixture and stirred for 2 h at room temperature and protected from light, after which the fluorescence intensity was measured. Seven more ions and small molecules (Hg2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Cr3+, dopamine (DA), polyglutamic acid (PGA)) were selected for specific determination of TC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of TC determination by FRET
ce:section.ce:para: The FRET mechanism constructed in this experiment uses the prepared CQDs as energy donors and CuNPs as energy acceptors, and the fluorescence transduction is based on the coincidence of the emission wavelength of the energy donors (CQDs) with the absorbance of the energy acceptors (CuNPs). Fig. 2 (a) shows the fluorescence spectra of the prepared CQDs with an excitation wavelength of 468 nm and an emission wavelength of 540 nm, and the inset of Fig. 2(a) shows the CQDs under UV light irradiation versus natural light irradiation. It can be seen that CQDs emit bright blue-green fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. Fig. 2(b) shows the overlap of the absorbance of CuNPs with the emission wavelength of the prepared CQDs, and it can be observed that there is an overlap between the emission wavelength of CQDs and the absorbance of CuNPs at the emission wavelength of 495 nm, which suggests that the fluorescence of the CQDs can be burst by the CuNPs through the FRET mechanism. After the addition of TC, TC can react with the surface groups of CuNPs, and coordinate with Cu2+ in CuNPs to form complexes, so that the fluorescence of CQDs can be recovered. The black line in Fig. 2(c) shows the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs with none added, the red line shows that the fluorescence of the CQDs was burst by the construction of the FRET mechanism after the addition of the CuNPs into the CQDs, and the blue line shows that after the addition of the TC, the TC formed a complex with the Cu2+ in the CuNPs, which recovered the fluorescence of the CQDs again.

To investigate the stability of the prepared CQDs, their photobleaching properties were investigated as shown in Fig. 3 . The prepared CQDs were continuously irradiated under 365 nm UV light for 1 h and their fluorescence intensity was measured every 5 min as shown in Fig. 3(a). Fig. 3(b) shows the fluorescence scatter plot of the photobleaching property of CQDs, which can be more obviously observed, and its fluorescence intensity did not decrease, indicating that the prepared CQDs are relatively stable. To further determine the stability of CQDs, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs was observed after one week of storage away from light, as shown in Fig. 3(c), and it can be seen that its fluorescence intensity decreased by about 200 after one week, which indicates that the prepared CQDs are more stable.

To further investigate the optical properties of the prepared CQDs, the fluorescence intensity of their emission wavelengths at different excitation wavelengths was measured, as shown in Fig. 4 (a). The excitation wavelengths in the range of 410–500 nm were selected for the measurements, and it can be seen that the fluorescence intensity is maximum when the excitation wavelength is located at 470 nm, while the excitation wavelengths between 410 and 470 nm can redshift the position of the emission peaks of CQDs. At excitation wavelengths between 470 and 500 nm, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs decreases, but the emission peak position does not change significantly. Fig. 4(b) shows the UV-Vis absorption spectra of the prepared CQDs, the absorbance of the CQDs in the region of 200–400 nm was measured, and it is observed that there is a unique absorption peak at 230 nm, which belongs to the π-π* leap of the aromatic C=C [31]. The intensity of the UV absorption peak of CQDs decreased gradually with the increase of dilution concentration, but the position of the absorption peak did not change significantly.

In this experiment, CQDs were prepared by microwave heating method using chenpi as a carbon source, to determine how the morphology of the prepared CQDs, i.e., the prepared CQDs were subjected to TEM scanning for observation. Fig. 5 (a) shows the morphology of CQDs under TEM, and it can be observed that the morphology of the prepared CQDs is spherical and uniformly distributed in size. Fig. 5(b) shows the particle size distribution of CQDs, which shows that the diameter of the prepared CQDs is distributed in the range of 1–3 nm with an average size of 1.91 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: X-ray diffraction analysis, FT-IR spectral analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the crystallization of the prepared CQDs, they were analyzed by XRD analysis, as shown in Fig. 6 (a), from which it can be seen that there is a diffraction peak of the prepared CQDs at 2θ = 21.3°, which indicates that the prepared CQDs correspond to the lattice spacing of the carbon material [32]. To explore the surface groups of the prepared CQDs, the FT-IR spectra was examined, as shown in Fig. 6(b), the FT-IR absorption bands of the CQDs were 3408 cm−1, 2923 cm−1, 1705 cm−1, 1616 cm−1, 1265–1439 cm−1, 1057–1161 cm−1, 781 cm−1, and 451–661 cm−1, respectively. The band at 3408 cm−1 is attributed to NH stretching vibrations [33]. The bands at 2923 cm−1 and 781 cm−1 are attributed to C−H stretching vibration [34]. The bands at 1705 cm−1 and 1616 cm−1 are attributed to the C=O stretching vibration [35,36]. The bands at 1265–1439 cm−1 are attributed to C−N, C−C, and C−O stretching vibrations, and the bands at 1057–1161 cm−1 are attributed to C−O−C, C−O, and C−S stretching vibrations [37]. The band at 451−661 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of C−S [38].

To investigate the elemental composition and surface groups of the prepared CQDs, they were subjected to XPS, as shown in Fig. 7 . The full spectral range is shown in Fig. 7(a), and three major peaks 285.5 eV, 399.5 eV, and 532.5 eV can be found, which correspond to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively, which suggests that the prepared CQDs mainly contain C, N, and O elements. Fig. 7(b) shows the high-resolution C 1s profile with three typical peaks of 284.7 eV, 286.1 eV, and 287.4 eV, corresponding to C=C, C−N [39], and C=O [40], respectively. Fig. 7(c) shows a high-resolution N 1s profile with the presence of two typical peaks mainly at 399.0 eV and 400.0 eV, which belong to C−N−C [41], and C−N [42], respectively. Fig. 7(d) shows a high-resolution O 1s profile with the main presence of 531.7 eV, 532.4 eV, and 533.4 eV, corresponding to C=O [43], C−OH/C−O−C [44], and C−O [45], respectively.

Based on the above studies of the prepared CQDs, the TCs were tested for linearity. TC in the concentration range of 1–100 nmol/L was prepared to observe the degree of bursting of the fluorescence intensity of the prepared CQDs after the addition of different concentrations of TC. Fig. 8 (a) shows the fluorescence spectra of CQDs after the addition of different concentrations of TC, and it can be observed that the fluorescence intensity increases with the increase of TC concentration. The TC concentration in the range of 1–100 nmol/L was selected for the experiment, and Fig. 8(b) shows the linear relationship between the TC concentration and the fluorescence intensity of CQDs for detecting this concentration range. From the figure, it can be seen that there is an extremely strong linear relationship between them, with a correlation coefficient of R 2 = 0.9874 and a determination limit of LOD = 0.3554 nmol/L. The results showed that the correlation coefficient was 0.9874.

To investigate the specificity of this method in TC determination, the method was applied to the determination of several other ionic small molecules, such as Hg2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Cr3+, DA, and PGA, under the optimal conditions determined during the above experiments. As shown in Fig. 9 , the fluorescence intensity was not significantly restored after the addition of other ionic small molecules. It can be observed that the fluorescence intensity of the added TC is higher than that of the other ionic small molecules, indicating that there is no interaction between the other ionic small molecules and the CuNPs to disrupt the FRET mechanism, and therefore the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs cannot be restored. The experimental results indicate that the method can be used for the determination of TC.

In conclusion, this work presents a novel approach for the rapid and sensitive determination of TC using CQDs and FRET. The CQDs were synthesized from chenpi, a readily available and inexpensive carbon source, via microwave synthesis, ensuring a green and efficient preparation process. The combination of CQDs with CuNPs created a FRET-based fluorescence sensor that selectively responded to TC, with a linear range of 1–100 nmol/L and a low determination limit of 0.36 nmol/L. This method displayed higher selectivity towards TC compared to other ionic small molecules, highlighting its specificity. The proposed CQDs fluorescent sensor offers a promising alternative for TC determination, contributing to advancements in analytical chemistry and environmental monitoring. Further studies could potentially expand the application of this technique to other analytes and explore additional strategies for improving sensor performance. Overall, this work demonstrates the potential of CQDs and FRET in the development of innovative sensing platforms for a variety of analytical needs.
All data and materials in this study are included in the published article and its additional file.
Ethics approval and consent to participate Not applicable.
All authors agree to publish.
The National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21864020), and the Science and Technology Planning Project of Inner Mongolia Autonomous Region (Grant No. 2021GG0367).

Ping LU: Writing – original draft, Software, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xiangru HOU: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Lu GA: Methodology, Conceptualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition. Gerile AODENG: Investigation, Methodology, Project administration. Jun AI: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Visualization, Validation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there is no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21864020 and 82160652), the Natural Science Foundation of Inner Mongolia (Grant Nos. 2019MS02014 and 2018MS02012), the ‘‘Young Science and Technology Talents Program’’ (Leading Person) in Inner Mongolia Autonomous Region Colleges and Universities (Grant No. NJYT-19-A04), the Collaborative Innovation Center for Water Environmental Security of Inner Mongolia Autonomous Region, China, (Grant No. XTCX003).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots: synthesis, properties and applications
J Mater Chem C

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang Y., Hu A. Carbon quantum dots: synthesis, properties and applications. J Mater Chem C, 2014, 2(34): 6921–39.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots as nanosensors for the spectrofluorimetric determination of meloxicam in biological fluids and dosage forms: greenness assessment and application to content uniformity testing
Sustain Chem Pharm

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Radwan A.S., Magdy G., Belal F., et al.
